ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mushroom matar (mushroom and green pea curry). FOODON_03311737 (mushroom food product) used as closest parent. A combined vegetable curry term is not available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked mushrooms as closest parent. No specific code exists for mushroom-and-pea curry preparation in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mushroom matar as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not routinely code composite curry dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of mushroom matar is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a medium restaurant bowl as typically served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mushroom (cooked) and Green Peas (cooked) component values; composite estimated from ICMR-NIN tables
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for mushroom matar as a composite dish. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition): cooked mushroom (~70% of dish by weight) and cooked green peas (~20%), with oil and spice contribution (~10%). Weighted composite calculated per 100 g edible portion. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) and cooked green peas (FDC ID 170420).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — green peas (GI ~48) and mushrooms (GI ~10–15); composite dish estimated
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mushroom matar as a composite dish. GI estimated as approximately 40 based on weighted average of primary ingredients: mushrooms (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database) and green peas (GI ~48, Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The low-GI vegetables, high fibre content, and protein from peas collectively moderate the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 14.4) / 100 = 5.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 420 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the meal, particularly when mushroom matar is served alongside other seasoned dishes such as dal or rice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (~40) and high dietary fibre from mushrooms and green peas
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mushroom matar has a low estimated GI of approximately 40 and a glycaemic load of approximately 5.8 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the dietary fibre (approximately 4.8 g per serving) and plant protein from green peas further support postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and rich in potassium and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mushroom matar is cholesterol-free with low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g) and provides potassium (~310 mg per 100 g) and dietary fibre (~2.4 g per 100 g), which collectively support cardiovascular health; preparation with minimal oil is recommended to keep total fat intake within heart-healthy limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom Matar (also written Mushroom Mutter or Mushroom Mattar) is a North Indian vegetarian curry dish made by cooking fresh button mushrooms and green peas together in a spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic in oil, then adding tomatoes and a blend of ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, before simmering the mushrooms and peas until tender. The result is a moist, lightly sauced dish with an earthy, umami-rich flavour from the mushrooms balanced by the natural sweetness of green peas and the warmth of Indian spices. Texture is soft with slight bite from the peas. Mushroom Matar is commonly served as a side dish with roti, naan, or steamed rice in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including North Indian establishments such as Maharani Table. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (~72 kcal per 100 g), low in saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and provides useful amounts of dietary fibre (~2.4 g per 100 g), plant protein (~3.8 g per 100 g), potassium, B vitamins, and iron. Its low estimated glycaemic index (~40) and low glycaemic load make it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is fully vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g) is moderate and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is mushroom matar good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for mushroom matar as a composite prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component tables for cooked mushrooms and cooked green peas with oil and spice contribution, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for the primary ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) component entries for cooked mushrooms and cooked green peas, weighted by approximate dish composition (~70% mushroom, ~20% peas, ~10% oil/spice/tomato/onion), and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central FDC IDs 169251 and 170420. No single approved-source entry for mushroom matar as a composite dish was found. 2. GI of ~40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mushroom matar exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the processing date. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-based dish prepared without hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN component data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 1.8 estimated from oil composition), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 estimated). 7. Sodium value of 210 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 8. The cuisine field is set to 'Indian' reflecting the dish's culinary origin; the input JSON listed 'Singapore' as cuisine, which reflects the serving context (field survey data). The dish is North Indian in origin and is served in Singapore by Indian restaurants. Multilingual names follow Singaporean mandatory language rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 9. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the ICMR-NIN component estimation approach used; where values could be reasonably estimated from component data they have been provided as positive numerics.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed. Identified that cuisine field needed clarification (input said Singapore, dish is North Indian origin served in Singapore). Set cuisine to Indian with region_of_origin noting Singapore serving context. Applied Singaporean multilingual rules as dish is served in Singapore.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Added மஷ்ரூம் மட்டர் to flat aliases to match Tamil aliases_by_language entry. Verified all zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) have zero-justification phrases in notes. Confirmed GI category 'low' is correct for GI=40 (≤55 threshold). Confirmed all -1 sentinel values are documented in notes. All hard gates and Q criteria verified as passing.
